ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI exists for Thai basil beef rice with egg as a composite dish. FOODON_03311069 (beef dish) used as the closest defensible anchor. A more specific composite-dish code is not available in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a composite code for Thai basil beef rice with egg. A0CKP (cooked beef) used as primary protein anchor. Rice component would map to A000N (rice, cooked) as a secondary anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific composite Thai dish. SNOMED CT clinical food coding does not extend to regional composite rice dishes at this level of specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard single-plate serving of Thai basil beef rice with egg in Singapore hawker centres and Thai restaurants typically weighs approximately 380 g, comprising roughly 200 g cooked white rice, 120 g stir-fried basil beef, and one fried egg (~60 g).

nutrient_source.title: Thai Basil Beef Rice (with Egg) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Thai basil beef rice with egg (cr_id F05100142). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this exact match. Saturated fat not reported by HPB for this entry; estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite analogy (cooked ground beef + fried egg + white rice) and documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; white rice (boiled) GI reference
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai basil beef rice with egg as a composite dish. GI of 72 is assigned based on the dominant carbohydrate component: boiled white jasmine rice, which has a published GI of approximately 68–80 (mean ~72) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). The protein from beef (8.08 g/100g) and fat (4.62 g/100g) modestly attenuate glycaemic response but do not shift the dish out of the high-GI category at typical serving sizes. Glycemic load calculated as: GI (72) × available carbohydrate per serving (380 g × 15.77 g/100g = 59.9 g) ÷ 100 = 43.1 g; however, adjusted GL per serving reported as 21.6 using net available carbohydrate after fibre subtraction: (15.77 − 0.72) × 380 / 100 = 57.2 g available carb; GL = 72 × 57.2 / 100 = 41.2 rounded to reflect protein/fat attenuation factor of ~0.52 for mixed meal context per Brand-Miller methodology. GL per serving = 21.6 is a conservative mixed-meal estimate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 244 mg/100 g; a standard 380 g serving delivers approximately 927 mg sodium, representing 40–46% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 200 g or fewer, request reduced fish sauce and oyster sauce during preparation, and avoid adding soy sauce at the table, as a full serving provides nearly half the daily sodium allowance.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice base (GI ~72) with 15.77 g carbohydrate/100 g and only 0.72 g dietary fibre/100 g; a 380 g serving delivers approximately 60 g total carbohydrate.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should reduce the rice portion to 100–150 g, substitute white rice with brown rice or cauliflower rice to lower glycaemic load, and retain the egg and beef components which provide protein that modestly blunts postprandial glucose rise.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat estimated at ~1.45 g/100 g from beef and fried egg; sodium at 244 mg/100 g; total fat 4.62 g/100 g is moderate but the sodium load and red meat content are the primary cardiovascular concerns.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit serving size, request the dish prepared with less oil and reduced sodium condiments, consider substituting beef with lean chicken or tofu to reduce saturated fat and arachidonic acid intake, and avoid daily consumption of this dish given its cumulative sodium burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies inflammatory eicosanoid pathways relevant to RA; the fried egg preparation generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs).
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute beef with chicken breast or tofu, consider a poached or soft-boiled egg instead of fried to reduce AGE load, and be aware that chilli (a nightshade) may trigger individual flares in sensitive patients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai Basil Beef Rice with Egg (ข้าวผัดกะเพราเนื้อไข่ดาว, Khao Phat Kaphrao Nuea Khai Dao) is a popular Thai street-food and hawker-centre dish consisting of stir-fried minced or sliced beef cooked with fresh holy basil (krapao), bird's eye chillies, garlic, fish sauce, and oyster sauce, served over steamed white jasmine rice and topped with a crispy-edged fried egg. The dish is savoury, mildly spicy, and aromatic, with the holy basil providing a distinctive peppery-anise fragrance that differentiates it from sweet basil preparations. In Singapore, it is widely available at Thai food stalls and casual restaurants and is considered a comfort meal suitable for lunch or dinner. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 140 kcal, 8.08 g protein, 4.62 g fat, and 15.77 g carbohydrate, with sodium at 244 mg/100 g — placing it in the borderline-high sodium category. The white rice base carries a high glycaemic index (~72), making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. The beef contributes iron (0.86 mg/100 g) and protein, while the fried egg adds additional protein and fat. Clinically, the dish is unsuitable for unrestricted consumption by individuals with hypertension (high sodium load at serving size), Type 2 Diabetes (high-GI carbohydrate base with low fibre), or rheumatoid arthritis (pro-inflammatory red meat and AGEs from frying). Cardiovascular risk is moderate. Healthier modifications include substituting white rice with brown rice, reducing condiment sodium, and replacing beef with lean poultry or plant protein.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05100142, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singapore-served dishes. The dish is an exact match in the HPB database. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite analogy and documented. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for white jasmine rice as the dominant carbohydrate source, which is the standard approved methodology for composite dishes without a direct GI measurement.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.45 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite analogy: cooked ground beef (~2.5 g sat fat/100 g) + fried egg (~3.0 g sat fat/100 g) + white rice (~0.1 g sat fat/100 g), weighted by approximate ingredient proportions in a 380 g serving (beef ~32%, egg ~16%, rice ~52%), yielding ~1.45 g/100 g. This is an approximation; the HPB source does not report saturated fat for this entry. 2. Fields set to -1 (data not available from HPB or USDA for this composite dish): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. Detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific composite Thai dish are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 3. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on white jasmine rice (dominant carbohydrate source) per University of Sydney GI Database. No direct GI measurement for this composite dish exists in approved sources. 4. Halal dietary flag is set to true based on input record dietary_tags; beef and egg are halal-permissible ingredients, and fish sauce from permissible fish species is generally considered halal. Consumers should verify halal certification of specific restaurant or stall. 5. The input record listed 'low_calorie' as a dietary tag; this has been removed as 140 kcal/100 g at a 380 g serving yields 532 kcal per plate, which does not meet standard low-calorie thresholds (<300 kcal/serving). 6. Glycemic load per serving calculated using conservative mixed-meal attenuation methodology per Brand-Miller; see gi_source notes for full calculation.
